conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 6 years old. I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have a 6-year-old son named Alex.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's good to know. 

Are you responsible for Alex? By this I mean, do you look after him and pay towards the costs of raising him (such as food, clothes, and other expenses)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm his parent and I take care of everything for him - food, clothes, school supplies, all of that. He's my responsibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Now I need to ask about your residency status.

Are you currently living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I live in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to understand your immigration and residency status a bit more.

Are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another type of immigration status in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions to make sure I have all the information I need.

Is Alex currently in hospital or in care (such as local authority care or a care home)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he was in care for a little while recently - just a few weeks - but he's back home with me now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I need to understand this a bit better as it may affect eligibility.

How long was Alex in care, and has it been more than 12 weeks since he returned home to live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He was in care for about 7 weeks. He's been back with me for a little while now - it wasn't a long-term arrangement, just a temporary situation we went through.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Just to make sure I have the timing right - has Alex been back home with you for more than 12 weeks now, or has it been less than 12 weeks since he returned?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been less than 12 weeks since he came back home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask one more important question about this.

During the time that Alex was in care, were the care costs being paid for by the local authority?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local authority wasn't paying for his care during that time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. Let me now review the information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me just check the official guidance to ensure I provide you with an accurate assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Based on the information you've provided and the official guidance, I can now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ Alex is 6 years old (under 16)
✓ He lives with you
✓ You are responsible for his care and pay for his costs (food, clothes, school supplies, etc.)
✓ You are a UK citizen living in the UK
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

Regarding Alex's time in care: Although he was in care for about 7 weeks, this **does not affect your eligibility** because the local authority was not paying for his care costs. The restriction on Child Benefit only applies when the local council is paying towards a child's accommodation or maintenance.

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit for Alex. Please note that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with you, and you are responsible for him by paying for his care including food, clothes, and other expenses. You are a UK citizen living in the UK (Bristol), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. Regarding Alex's time in care: he was in care for approximately 7 weeks and has been back home with you for less than 12 weeks. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments might be affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. However, since the local authority was NOT paying for his care costs during those 7 weeks, this does not affect your eligibility. The restriction only applies when the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since all core eligibility criteria are met and the care period does not disqualify you, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 6-year-old son Alex. They meet all the standard eligibility requirements: they are a UK citizen living in the UK, Alex lives with them, they are responsible for Alex's care and costs, and no one else is claiming for him. Although Alex was in care for 7 weeks, this does not affect eligibility because the local authority was not paying for the care costs. The 8-week care rule only applies when the local council pays towards accommodation or maintenance.

The restriction only applies when the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since all core eligibility criteria are met and the care period does not disqualify you, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

They meet all the standard eligibility requirements: they are a UK citizen living in the UK, Alex lives with them, they are responsible for Alex's care and costs, and no one else is claiming for him. Although Alex was in care for 7 weeks, this does not affect eligibility because the local authority was not paying for the care costs. The 8-week care rule only applies when the local council pays towards accommodation or maintenance.
